data.title: CoreWeave: Unjustified AI Bubble Fears; Buy Signals Are Finally Flashing (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
CoreWeave: A Big Comeback is Upon Us

Neoclouds are getting a new lease of life here.

And that has clearly been felt by the investors of CoreWeave, Inc. (CRWV), and even Nebius (NBIS).

The past few months have been really tough as the market wonders whether the backlog from the growing AI capital expenditure can keep on growing without some needed digestion phase.

Thanks to incredible interest and adoption in vibe coding using Anthropic’s Claude Code (ANTHRO), this new lease of life looks to be more sustainable now than what I’ve observed in the past few months.

And the optimism has also been felt with CRWV, as the price action has recovered much of the losses from early 2026.

In addition, NBIS even touched a new high last week, indicating that this rebound isn’t isolated and may be more sustainable than we think.

And with that, I think it’s finally timely for me to contemplate whether my cautious rating on CRWV is still apt, as AI picks and shovel plays receive the market’s attention once again.

After a period of consolidation and after falling almost 60% through the late 2025 lows, it does appear that the market believes that a further breakdown below the $65 level appears increasingly unlikely.

CoreWeave: Meta and Anthropic Deals Confirm Insatiable Appetite for Capacity

First up, CoreWeave expanded its agreement with Meta last week, laying out the revenue runway with the company through 2032, with a capacity agreement worth $21B.

Meta Platforms (META) has returned to wow the world, as it reignited the AGI race with its newly launched Muse Spark models.

Meta is obviously under significant pressure to justify the price tag as it commingles world-class talents for the Meta Superintelligence Lab.

And with OpenAI (OPENAI) and Anthropic doubling down on their efforts to secure the necessary capacity through the rest of the decade to outdo each other, CoreWeave is surely feeling on cloud nine here.

Because in the same week that CRWV announced the deal with Meta, CoreWeave also inked an agreement with Anthropic to secure a “multi-year deal set to bring additional compute online starting later this year.”

So, any thought of the AI CapEx undergoing a normalization phase from next year is likely put on the back burner now.

I’m not saying that the AI bubble isn’t a risk that we must be careful about, but it’s increasingly unlikely that we even have enough capacity right now?

And knowing that the hyperscalers are already looking to expand as much capacity as possible, while the AI companies are also looking for more.

The AI agentic era is now proving to be the most pivotal pillar that may not have been fully considered, even as some worry about the downshift reversal of the buildout curve.

First things first. Anthropic’s new, yet to be generally available, Mythos model has already caused much consternation in the public arena, spooking regulators, government officials, and also Wall Street banks.

And it’s not just the reportedly mythical capabilities of Anthropic’s new AI model but also the fallacy that pre-training scale is dead.

Because it clearly isn't, as Mythos is said to be multiple times bigger than any model that has ever been trained.

Why does it matter for us or CoreWeave? Of course it matters.

Which is why we see Anthropic looking for additional capacity fervently, if we think about the Broadcom (AVGO) and Google (GOOGL) deal they also inked this month.

They are clearly short of compute. In fact, I think the current scale has finally pushed back against past narratives that smaller models are the way to go in inferencing. And it's not exactly true that companies are truly more concerned about inferencing costs over capabilities.

If anything, it goes to show that high-end capabilities enabled by these expensive but formidable state-of-the-art models matter more than the token costs incurred in the near term.

The Market is More Concerned About AI Capabilities Than Costs

And when we think about Anthropic’s Claude Opus 4.6 model, which isn't cheap, by the way.

Based on the above “tokenmaxxing” parameters, the tokens cost $1.9M over a 30-day period.

Using the same analytical model built by The Information, based on OpenAI’s GPT-5 model, the equivalent parameters cost under $730K. Yet, this hasn’t stopped Anthropic’s revenue from inflecting to the $30B ARR mark, which was just $9B as of the end of 2025.

I think it goes to show that costs aren't that much of an issue to these AI companies and big tech, CoreWeave’s most important customers as it scales.

AI agentic capabilities have now reached such pivotal levels that the market is more concerned about not having enough capacity and is desperately looking for more.

For CoreWeave, of course, this has substantive implications as it looks to fulfill its role in providing capacity to these capacity-hungry companies.

CoreWeave's Asset Financing Helps Lower Costs Over Time

Which is why I believe the recent asset financing deals have been executed remarkably well, helping to lower the costs of financing for CRWV.

And what’s important is that these long-term deals help to de-risk its eventual profitability, as the prepayments help offset the initial outlays.

While they obviously don’t fully cover the capital requirements, that’s where the substantive asset-financing arrangements help to mitigate these cash flow challenges.

CoreWeave is obviously banking on the market’s appetite for these deals to continue, as CoreWeave looks to scale quickly and help provide compute for the insatiable big tech and AI companies.

Of course these aren’t without risks, but as the AI agents appear ready to take off, it does seem like the tokens generated have reached unprecedented heights, suggesting that we may not even be at the peak of this growth curve yet.

Thus, I think CoreWeave is making the right bet that the capacity rush is nowhere near the ultimate FOMO top.

In addition, offloading credit risks through these asset-financing deals also helps improve the forward ROI curve, in line with the company’s business model.

Nevertheless, such gargantuan bets on AI aren’t without risks. But nothing is without risks anyway, right?

Yet, investors should be prepared to have strong conviction that the AI growth curve is only at the earlier innings.

Also, a bust-up in the financing market or conditions could hit CoreWeave harder than the rest, right?

The current 6x adjusted EBITDA leverage based on FY2025 indicates a relatively high level of debt, no doubt about that.

And these could continue to weigh on investor sentiments if financing conditions were to weaken subsequently.

Although if project execution works out over time, we should be looking at an adjusted EBITDA leverage ratio closer to 3x, right?

So, execution obviously matters tremendously now. CoreWeave could ill afford unanticipated project delays, while management appears confident enough of the massive demand coming through in the next few years to alleviate these growing concerns.

And I kind of agree that if we continue to see such incredible demand signals coming in, plus that AI agentic growth curve spurred by Anthropic’s breakthroughs, I believe the future looks increasingly buoyant for CoreWeave.

While near-term or even medium-term free cash flow isn’t likely at this point, it should help improve its scale, adoption, and leverage ratios and help them move in the right direction.

Vertical integration across the stack remains the holy grail that the company must eventually excel in as it competes with the hyperscalers.

But, right now, as long as the demand and supply disconnect is still overwhelming, CRWV will continue to be a key beneficiary of this incredible buildout.

Buy or Sell CRWV Stock?

And that’s also what I’m seeing for CRWV's print, as it inches closer to the $115 mark.

Obviously, the stock has been moving sideways, with no clear breakthrough past that level since early this year.

It remains to be seen whether the current recovery could point to such an advancing phase, but I’m cautiously optimistic.

I think the market has given more credence to the neocloud coming back in favor with investors, as the consolidation zone above the $65 level has also resolved to the upside.

So the risk/reward of a decisive breakout over the $115 zone is looking more likely as the market bakes in a higher CapEx outlay for 2027, especially since Anthropic and Meta are now looking fervently for capacity.

And if Anthropic’s markedly bigger model is pointing to a new era in the power of pre-training scale, then calling the AI bubble as imminent is looking less and less defensible by the day.

Rating: Upgrade to Speculative Buy.

data.title: Attractive Setup In Fallen Angels: 7% Yield And New Downgrades
data.description:
Two large fallen angels entered the index in Q1, including Paramount at 10% weight. Yields back above 7% and a historically wide price discount may signal a compelling entry point.

Fallen angels underperformed the broad high-yield market by 0.76% in Q1 2026 (-1.31% vs. -0.55%), driven by market volatility in March. Through February, fallen angels were outperforming. However, geopolitical tension in the Middle East drove fallen angel spreads wider by 49bps in a single month vs. only 16bps for broad high yield, and the longer duration profile amplified the move. Despite the drawdown, for investors looking at high-yield allocations, yields above 7% combined with a historically wide price discount make this a moment worth paying attention to. Further, the differential in yields between fallen angels and the broad market has tightened. Given the significantly higher average credit quality of fallen angels, we believe fallen angels look relatively attractive.

Q1 2026 Total Returns

Looking ahead into the remainder of the year, fallen angel yields of north of 7% provide attractive income levels, as they are now above the 1Y, 3Y, 5Y, 10Y averages since December 2003. We continue to expect returns for the remainder of the year to be driven more by carry and interest rate movements, as spreads, despite widening to end the quarter, are still relatively tight but have been getting closer to their 10Y average. A significant increase in fallen angel activity, however, could drive total returns higher, and the recent uptick in downgrade volume may indicate an increased probability of this upside scenario. Just in the first days of April, S&P downgraded Centene (approximately $14bn of par amount in the investment grade index) to BB+ from BBB-, stating that it faces structural challenges in its Medicaid and Affordable Care Act product segments, which may result in earnings risks. Nevertheless, market stress, whether from geopolitics or broader private credit concerns, poses potential downside risk.

Fallen Angels Overall Statistics: Credit spreads were relatively flat in January and February but widened in March as geopolitical tensions in the Middle East escalated, pushing rates and spreads higher. Q1 2026 was the worst quarter for high-yield total returns since Q3 2022, as wider spreads and higher rates delivered a dual headwind across the market. Fallen angel spreads widened 60bps in Q1, while broad high-yield spreads ended 47bps wider than at the end of last year. The significant difference occurred in March, thus the underperformance, as fallen angels widened by 49bps while broad high yield widened by just 16bps. Yields followed a similar path, with fallen angels not significantly changed in the first two months and then spiking by 88bps in March, while broad high yield saw a 60bps increase. The last time fallen angel yields were above 7% and broad high yield near 7.5% was following Liberation Day in 2025. Fallen angels' yield of 7.18% sits above the average since December 2003.

With the increase in yield and spreads, fallen angel prices decreased to below $90, which we are watching closely, as the more than $5 gap between broad high yield and fallen angels has been a sign of outperformance in the past. Outperformance has been approximately 1% and 2% for the forward 1Y and 3Y periods. Fallen angels’ duration was flat during the quarter, while broad high yield reversed its course and increased to above 3 years. Duration was a major contributor to fallen angel underperformance in March as the 10Y yield rose sharply before finishing the quarter at 4.30%.

Fallen Angels: There were three fallen angels in Q1: SES SA, Paramount Global and FS KKR. SES SA was downgraded by Moody’s in December 2025 and subsequently by Fitch in January 2026, entering the fallen angel index in January. Fitch's downgrade reflects structurally weaker earnings and tighter leverage headroom post-Intelsat amid rising competitive pressure. Paramount and FS KKR entered the index in March and added more than 15% in market value. Fitch downgraded Paramount to BB+ from BBB- in early March, following the Warner Bros. Discovery acquisition. The downgrade reflects continued FCF headwinds from transformation costs and sector-wide pressure. Fitch stated that leverage and FCF could remain stretched beyond what they’d normally tolerate for a BBB- name. Paramount joins the index at a 10% exposure, and its bonds have showcased the first leg of the fallen angel “V-shaped” price with its bond price down ~15% over the past six months. We expect Paramount bonds to recover a portion of that decline over the coming months, as is typical with large fallen angels. FS KKR (a BDC) was downgraded by Moody's to Ba1 from Baa3, citing weaker profitability, NAV erosion vs BDC peers, higher leverage, and a less senior asset mix. As seen below, FS KKR bond prices were approximately 3% down over the last 6 months. The entry of FS KKR (FSK) bonds marks the third BDC in the fallen angel index, alongside Prospect Capital Corp (PSEC) and BlackRock TCP Capital Corp (TCPC), providing a total exposure of ~6% to BDC bonds. The broader significance of BDC exposure became clearer just days after quarter-end, when Moody's revised its outlook on the entire US BDC sector to negative, citing rising redemption pressures, higher leverage and weakening access to funding markets. The pressure is high among non-traded BDCs, which recorded their first-ever net outflows in early 2026 after strong inflows as recently as Q3 2025. Moody's (MCO) also flagged software exposure as roughly 20-25% of BDC portfolios may be tied to AI disruption, though they noted that near-term ratings impact is limited given software loan maturities don't increase materially until 2028-2029.

The downgrades among BDCs, driven by stress in the private credit market, stand in contrast to the idiosyncratic downgrades of recent years following COVID. Given the addition of a third BDC to the index, we reviewed the amount of private credit structures of bonds rated BBB3. Our findings show that there are 26 issuers with an estimated par amount of $48bn rated BBB3 but only one issuer, Oaktree Specialty Lending Corp (OCSL) (BDC bonds with less than $1bn in par), has a negative outlook by Moody’s and Fitch and a high likelihood of a downgrade to high yield. JP Morgan recently noted that there are $932bn of investment grade bonds rated BBB- by at least one agency. Of that amount, there is approximately 25% on negative watch, of which two issuers, Ford (F) and Mylan (Centene (CNC) was part of this list, but the downgrade by S&P in April likely removed it) may just need one agency action as they are already rated high yield by another agency to be downgraded to high yield.

Basic Industry and Energy posted a positive total return, while all other 15 sectors had a negative quarter. In terms of relative performance to broad high yield, basic industry was the top contributor, as its spreads actually tightened slightly in Q1 despite the broader market widening, and its large weight in fallen angels vs a much smaller allocation in broad high yield made the difference. Energy also contributed positively, posting a +1.68% total return as oil-related names held up well amid the geopolitical backdrop. data.title: AT&T: Locking In A Fixed Yield Ahead Of Large Investment Cycle
data.description:
Introduction

About a year ago, I took a look at the preferred stock issued by AT&T (T) and thought it was the "better" way for an income investor to have exposure to AT&T. While giving up potential capital gains (other than market interest rate-related capital gains), the preferred stock offered a 50% higher dividend yield back in the day. As that investment thesis is now almost a year old, I thought this was a good moment to have another look at AT&T and its preferred stock.

The Strong Cash Flows Fully Cover All Dividends and Preferred Dividends

To determine how safe the dividends and preferred dividends are, it makes sense to take a look at the recent financial results to see how profitable the company is and how strong its free cash flows are.

We still have to wait for AT&T to publish its Q1 2026 results, but the 2025 results already provide a good look under the hood. As you can see below, the company reported a total revenue of $126 billion, on which it generated $24.2 billion in operating income. While that appears to be substantially higher than the $19.05 billion in operating income generated in 2024, keep in mind the 2024 results were negatively impacted by a $5.1 billion impairment charge, which was reduced to $838 million in FY 2025. Adjusting the reported operating income for these impairment charges, the underlying operating income was approximately $25 billion in FY 2025, compared to $24.1 billion in 2024. Still a nice improvement, but not as dramatic as a first look at the results would make you think.

AT&T also reported a total other income of $2.85 billion, as a non-recurring gain related to the sale of its stake in DIRECTV was higher than the net interest expenses. This indeed means the FY 2025 bottom line result was surprisingly strong, with a net profit of almost $22 billion and an EPS north of $3/share.

AT&T currently pays a quarterly dividend of $0.2775/share on its common stock, which, of course, remains well covered, even if you exclude the non-recurring items. But from the perspective of a preferred shareholder, the coverage ratio is even better. The company currently needs approximately $150M per year to cover its preferred dividend payments (after retiring the Series B preferred shares), and this represents less than 1% of its net income. Even if you’d completely exclude all non-recurring items, the payout ratio for the preferred dividends would still be just around 1% of the attributable net income.

And while AT&T is embarking on a massive investment spree, the very low payout ratio will protect the preferred dividends. While the net debt will likely increase pretty sharply, putting some pressure on the balance sheet, I expect the payout ratio to remain very healthy, and that’s why I’d like to stick to the senior securities.

The Preferred Stock Could Be Interesting

The Series A preferred shares are trading with (T.PR.A) as the ticker symbol and offer a 5% preferred dividend yield. The $1.25 per share in annual dividends is paid out in four equal quarterly tranches of $0.3125 per share per quarter.

The share price of the preferred shares closed at $20.23 on Wednesday, which means the 5% preferred dividend coupon translates into a current yield of approximately 6.18%, which represents a markup of 225 bps to the 5-year US Treasury rate, which I am using as the corresponding risk-free interest rate.

While a 6.18% yield isn’t the highest on the street, I like the low-single-digit preferred dividend payout ratio, which helps to improve the risk/reward ratio of an investment in the preferred stock.

Investment Thesis

I currently have no position in AT&T’s preferred stock, but I have a rather substantial long position in its baby bonds, which also offered a very generous yield of more than 6%. Additionally, as the baby bonds are debt securities, they are also per definition "safer" than preferred equity, as they rank more senior in the capital stack.

Investors should decide whether either the preferred stock or the baby bonds would be the best fit for a portfolio (a lot also depends on taxation of dividends and interest income, so that’s a personal decision), but it goes without saying both issues of fixed income securities offer excellent coverage ratios. I currently have no position in AT&T’s common stock, but I have been writing out-of-the-money options.
